mnemonics.title: HYPERVISOR TYPES
mnemonics.content:
TYPE-1 = BARE-METAL (Runs directly on hardware)
TYPE-2 = HOSTED (Runs on host OS)
Mnemonic: ONE-ONE IS ALONE (Type-1 stands alone without host), TWO IS TOO (Type-2 needs TWO - host + hypervisor)

mnemonics.title: VIRTUALIZATION BENEFITS
mnemonics.content:
S C A L D E
S - Server Consolidation
C - Cost Reduction
A - Application Isolation
L - Load Balancing
D - Disaster Recovery
E - Easy Backup/Snapshot
Mnemonic: SCALDE helps SAVE resources

acronyms.usage: The core component that controls hardware and allocates resources to guest operating systems
Software implementation of a physical computer that executes programs like a real machine
Intel processor extension for hardware-assisted virtualization
A packaging format for virtual appliances containing VM image and metadata
An open standard for packaging and distributing virtual appliances
Physical memory in host machine that is allocated to VMs
Physical processor that is virtualized and allocated to VMs

visualTips.visualization: Draw a simple diagram: BOTTOM = Hardware, MIDDLE = Hypervisor (layer), TOP = Multiple VMs side-by-side. For Type-1, hypervisor sits directly on hardware. For Type-2, add one more layer: Hardware -> Host OS -> Hypervisor -> VMs.
Think of physical hardware as a PIZZA. Each VM gets a SLICE (virtual CPU, virtual RAM, virtual disk). The hypervisor is the PIZZA CUTTER that divides resources fairly.
Imagine moving furniture from one house to another WITHOUT stopping anyone using it. Memory pages fly like flying carpets - some items move, some stay in use, then final switch happens.
Full Virt = BLINDFOLDED driver (OS doesn't know it's virtual, hypervisor intercepts everything). Para-virt = AWARE driver (knows about hypervisor, communicates directly via hypercalls).

analogies.analogy: A VM is like a RENTAL APARTMENT in a building. The building (hardware) is shared, each apartment (VM) has its own rooms (virtual resources), and the building manager (hypervisor) handles utilities areas.
The hypervisor is like an AIR TRAFFIC CONTROL SYSTEM. It manages multiple planes (VMs) landing and taking off (running), allocating runways (resources), and ensuring no collisions.
A snapshot is like a PHOTO of your room - it captures exactly how everything looks at that moment. You can clean up (make changes), and if you don't like it, you can restore to the photo.

Memory overcommitment is like a RESTAURANT with 100 seats but accepts 150 reservations. They rely on the fact that not everyone comes at exactly the same time - some tables get shared, some guests wait.
VM isolation is like ISOLATED SAFETY CAGES in a laboratory. Each experiment (VM) happens in its own cage - if one explodes (crashes), it doesn't affect others in the building.
